story_title: The London Gold Fix Scandal — 95 Years of Benchmark Secrecy Exposed
summary: In 2014, regulators in the UK, US, and Europe opened investigations into manipulation of the century-old London Gold Fix — a twice-daily phone call between five banks that set the global benchmark price for gold — leading to the abolition of the old Fix and its replacement with the LBMA Gold Price, an electronic auction run by the ICE Benchmark Administration.
recommended_arc: Hook: five banks on a conference call have been setting the world price of gold every business day since 1919. Rising: a Bloomberg investigation and academic papers reveal statistical anomalies — prices moved suspiciously in the minutes around the Fix. Climax: the FCA and US DOJ launch probes; Deutsche Bank exits and pays $60 million to settle. Payoff: the old Fix is replaced by a transparent electronic auction in 2015.

verified_facts.claim: The London Gold Fix was established in 1919 when five bullion dealers — including N M Rothschild & Sons, Mocatta & Goldsmid, Pixley & Abell, Samuel Montagu & Co., and Sharps Wilkins — first met at Rothschild's office in New Court, London to set a daily benchmark price for gold.

By 2013, the Fix was conducted twice daily (10:30 AM and 3:00 PM London time) by five banks: Barclays, Deutsche Bank, HSBC, Bank of Nova Scotia, and Société Générale; the benchmark was used to value an estimated $20 trillion in gold-linked financial products and gold contracts annually.

In 2014, a Bloomberg News investigation identified statistically anomalous price patterns in the minutes around the daily 3:00 PM Fix, including an unusual frequency of price moves that appeared to benefit parties holding large derivative positions.

Deutsche Bank announced in January 2014 that it would withdraw from the London Gold Fix, citing the regulatory environment; it left the Fix in May 2014, the same month the UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) imposed a £26 million fine on Barclays for failing to manage a conflict of interest during a Gold Fix in 2012.

In April 2016, Deutsche Bank agreed to pay $60 million to settle a US class-action lawsuit alleging manipulation of the London Gold Fix between 2004 and 2013, and agreed to cooperate with plaintiffs by sharing communications and data about other banks' alleged conduct.

The LBMA Gold Price — an electronic, audited auction administered by ICE Benchmark Administration — replaced the century-old London Gold Fix on 20 March 2015, with a daily afternoon auction expanded from five to a broader set of accredited participants.
